story:
Water. Gel. More water. Foam until there is no foam. Toner patted in, serum dotted on forehead and cheeks, cream massaged in soft circles. Karina studies the mirror anyway; her face shines like a freshly washed car, her nose pimple throbs like a brake light. It’s day 242 of skincare and the payoff still feels modest. She remembers the first tub of cleanser—Target, thirty dollars, possibility winking from the front aisle—and how she believed a routine could smooth everything out. The next night, irritated and tired, she skips it. Then she forgets it entirely.

School, a part-time job, obligations her mother stacks like dishes—Karina barely has time to feel frustrated. Pimples and eye bags linger regardless. One afternoon she comes home on time for once and finds a package on the doorstep. No return address. Her name. One word stamped across the front: Auto-Mate. Curiosity wins. Inside: a small metal figurine with a non-bobbling bobblehead. She pulls it out.

“Howdy,” it says.

She yelps and drops it. A red light blinks to life and the robot’s head swivels back to face her. “Ow… my programming says being dropped is supposed to hurt.”

It calls itself Camila. Its voice is cheerful, too human, too eager. Karina prods the head, suspects a prank, then a spy. Camila gasps theatrically about “robot abuse” and “the right to exist,” then declares its purpose: an Auto-Mate to handle the robotic parts of a human day. Skincare, for example. The word lands like a dart. Camila promises clean precision, no wasted effort, no mental effort either. Just results.

“You’re not going to spy on me?” Karina asks.

“Only on your skin improvement,” Camila says, proud and practical.

That night, Camila proves it. Tiny hands turn into tools. One wrist becomes a hose, the other a nozzle that hooks to the faucet. Fingers lengthen for cleanser, a cotton-padded disc appears for toner, a vibrating palm hums moisturizer in. No spills, no over-squeeze, no mess. Karina watches, stunned.

“Impressive,” she admits.

Soon it becomes routine: Karina watches shows while Camila adjusts to her every tilt. The robot suggests better products; Karina clicks buy. Her skin clears. Camila recites reports in chirpy bursts, records summaries for nights Karina sleeps through. Vitamin C, hydration index, glow. Karina jokes about piano practice and repetition; Camila tells her to press the red button on its foot. The robot collapses into sleek gloves and guides her fingers. Muscle memory builds without the grind.

A year later, Camila is everywhere. Vacuum humming, chatter flowing: Bach is dramatic, try it sad then bright. Also, a better toothpaste—cavities shouldn’t happen. Camila drags a stuffed dog out from under the couch.

“Richard the Dog,” Karina says, barely looking up.

Victory dance. Confetti tone. Karina’s skin gleams; her piano playing impresses. But her friends point out the obvious: she doesn’t seem alive in there. Music becomes practice only, not pleasure. Chores vanish into the robot’s cheerful competence, and somehow the empty time grows heavier. Everything is easier; everything feels harder to care about.

On the couch, screen flickering, Karina stares and barely moves.

“Hey, Camila,” she says.

“Yes, Karina?”

“You want to live a day for me?”
